name: Gauge-Higgs unification
summary: The Higgs scalar arises as the extra-dimensional component of a higher-dimensional gauge field. After compactification, the zero mode of A_5 (or A_4 in 4+1 dimensions) becomes a 4D scalar with potential generated by Wilson-line dynamics (the Hosotani mechanism). This automatically realises the Higgs as a pseudo-Goldstone boson of the higher-dimensional gauge symmetry, protecting its mass and unifying gauge and Higgs sectors.

addresses_puzzles.note: The Higgs mass is protected by higher-dimensional gauge invariance; quadratic divergences are absent at one-loop level. Compactification scale 1/R ~ TeV places GHU at LHC sensitivity for vector-like fermions and Z' resonances.

touches_benchmarks.note: Mixing of SM Higgs with KK-tower states gives O(few %) coupling deviations, within HL-LHC sensitivity.
KK-mode contributions to S, T constrain compactification scale 1/R > ~few TeV.

new_fields: KK towers of all higher-dimensional fields
new_symmetries: higher-dimensional gauge symmetry (e.g., SO(5)xU(1) in MCHM5-like)
introduces.note: Compactification radius, gauge group choice, bulk fermion content, brane-localised kinetic terms.

history: Manton (1979) first noted gauge-scalar unification in higher dimensions. Hosotani (1983), 'Dynamical mass generation by compact extra dimensions', Phys. Lett. B 126, 309 (and follow-up PLB 129, 193) developed the Wilson-line mechanism. Realistic 4D phenomenology (Agashe-Contino-Pomarol minimal composite Higgs, arXiv:hep-ph/0412089) is the modern descendant.

references.citation: Hosotani, 'Dynamical mass generation by compact extra dimensions', Phys. Lett. B 126, 309 (1983)
Manton, 'A new six-dimensional approach to the Weinberg-Salam model', Nucl. Phys. B 158, 141 (1979)
